strands.content: User is refactoring a Flask auth system currently scattered across app.py (login/logout routes), models.py (User + password hashing), decorators.py (@login_required), api/routes.py (API key validation), and jobs/worker.py (service account tokens). The proposed consolidated architecture centralizes all auth into an auth/ package with clear separation of concerns: models (User, APIKey, ServiceAccount), service layer (AuthService with all business logic), middleware (single request identity resolution), decorators (permission checks only), and exceptions (typed auth errors). The target is to establish the principle that middleware resolves identity ONCE per request and decorators only check permissions, eliminating scattered parsing logic. AuthService is the single entry point for all authentication flows (password, API key, OAuth2) and should never be circumvented.

key_claims.text: Middleware should resolve identity once per request, populating g.current_user and g.auth_context, while decorators only verify permissions
AuthService should be a single instance handling all auth logic (web session, API key, service account, OAuth) in one place
API keys should use prefix.secret format with plaintext prefix for lookup and hashed secret for security
Exceptions should be typed (InvalidCredentialsError, ExpiredTokenError, RevokedKeyError, OAuthError) and carry HTTP status codes
OAuth provider errors (502) should be distinguished from user credential errors (401) to avoid confusing clients

strands.title: OAuth Account Linking & Storage
strands.content: User is implementing OAuth2 without the full refactor. Core requirement: support multiple OAuth providers (Google, GitHub, Microsoft, etc.) linked to a single User account. Schema uses `user_oauth_accounts` table with three identity columns: user_id (FK), provider (e.g., 'google', 'github'), and provider_user_id (the stable OAuth identity, never email). The unique constraint on (provider, provider_user_id) prevents account takeover if email gets recycled or social account reused. Tokens (access, refresh) are stored in Text columns nullable for flexibility; token_expires_at supports automatic refresh. is_active boolean allows soft-delete (user revokes a single provider link without affecting account). Relationship back to User via oauth_accounts list. Indexes: one on user_id (account management queries), one on (provider, provider_user_id) for the OAuth callback hot path. OAuthAccount model includes is_token_expired() check and revoke() method that scrubs tokens. User model has get_oauth_account(provider) convenience method for checking if a provider is linked. Migration uses server_default for booleans and timestamps to ensure safety on replay. ForeignKeyConstraint has ondelete='CASCADE' so user deletion cleans up OAuth rows. Critical for performance: the (provider, provider_user_id) lookup to find the user is O(1) indexed but requires caching because it will be a hot path during OAuth login flows.

key_claims.text: provider_user_id is the canonical OAuth identity, not email, to prevent account takeover from email recycling
UniqueConstraint on (provider, provider_user_id) prevents two users from linking the same provider identity
Tokens are stored as nullable Text, not required String, to accommodate PKCE and flows that don't issue refresh tokens
is_active soft-delete allows user to revoke one provider link without affecting their account or audit history
ForeignKeyConstraint with ondelete='CASCADE' ensures OAuth rows are cleaned up when user is deleted
Two separate indexes (user_id lookup and provider_user_id lookup) are needed; unique constraint alone doesn't cover both query patterns
server_default in migration for booleans and timestamps ensures data safety if migration is replayed

strands.title: User Auth State Caching Strategy — Three-Layer Architecture
strands.content: To handle multiple auth lookup paths (session → user, API key → user, OAuth provider identity → user) without redundant DB queries, implement a three-layer Redis cache: (1) Canonical user cache keyed by user_id with 5-minute TTL, stores only auth-critical fields (id, is_active, username, roles) via User.to_cache_dict() and User.from_cache_dict(), (2) API key pointer cache keyed by SHA256(raw_key) with 1-hour TTL, stores just the user_id, (3) OAuth pointer cache keyed by 'oauth:{provider}:{provider_user_id}' with 1-hour TTL, stores just the user_id. All three layers use Redis GETs on the hot path. Canonical user cache hits require only Redis, no DB. Pointer cache hits require one Redis GET (pointer) + one Redis GET (user), still faster than DB JOIN. Cold caches are populated on first access: API key lookup queries the ApiKey table (indexed by key_hash), OAuth lookup queries user_oauth_accounts table (indexed by provider + provider_user_id), both set their pointer mapping for future TTL window. Invalidation rules are strict: any user mutation (disable, profile update, role change) calls invalidate_user(user_id), which clears the canonical cache; API key revocation or OAuth account unlink also clear their respective pointer caches. Pointer staleness is acceptable (they're just user_id references), but canonical cache staleness is not (user state must be current). Pointer TTL is longer than user TTL because a stale pointer pointing to a fresh user is safe; the next canonical lookup on the pointer target will repopulate. Never cache full OAuth token responses or bearer tokens in this layer — separate session/token layer handles those. Multi-process (gunicorn) workers each get their own Redis connection and see the same cache, so invalidation is cluster-wide. No pub/sub needed; Redis is the source of truth.

key_claims.text: Three Redis layers (user, apikey pointer, oauth pointer) avoid database joins on hot paths
Canonical user cache (user_id) has 5-minute TTL; pointer caches (apikey, oauth) have 1-hour TTL
Pointer-to-user lookup pattern: Redis GET pointer → user_id → Redis GET user → auth decision, zero DB queries on cache hits
invalidate_user() clears canonical cache only; pointers naturally point to refreshed user on next canonical lookup
OAuth (provider, provider_user_id) hot-path lookup is O(1) indexed DB query, cached behind pointer for 1 hour
Multi-process workers share Redis cache cluster-wide; no pub/sub invalidation needed

depends_on: Disabled User API Key Bug & Fix

strands.title: Redis Shared Instance with Namespace Isolation
strands.content: The auth cache can share the existing Redis instance used by the job queue, satisfying the ops rule of no new infrastructure for minor releases. Critical requirement: strict key namespacing to prevent collisions and entanglement. Define a RedisNamespace enum with values JOB_QUEUE='jq', AUTH_CACHE='ac', SESSION='ss', and a make_key(namespace, *parts) function that constructs hierarchical keys like 'ac:token:user_456', 'ac:oauth:google:provider_user_id', 'jq:task:abc123'. Never concatenate keys ad-hoc; all key construction must go through make_key(). Auth cache TTLs: AUTH_TOKEN_TTL=3600 (1 hour), AUTH_OAUTH_TTL=300 (5 min, shorter for sensitive OAuth state), AUTH_NEGATIVE_TTL=60 (cache explicit misses to prevent DB hammering on brute-force or malformed tokens). AuthCache class wraps redis_client and exposes high-level methods (get_user_for_token, set_user_for_token, set_negative) that handle JSON serialization and negative-cache detection (raw value '__miss__' signals a known-bad token without going to DB). Namespace isolation must be confirmed with ops team before deploy: redis-cli --scan --pattern 'ac:*' should return empty on existing prod instance.

key_claims.text: Redis instance can be shared between job queue and auth cache via namespace prefixes
Key construction must always use make_key(RedisNamespace, *parts); ad-hoc concatenation is a critical bug
Negative cache (set_negative) prevents DB hammering on bad tokens; TTL=60s is sufficient because tokens are checked on every request
AUTH_TOKEN_TTL=3600 and AUTH_OAUTH_TTL=300 balance cache hit rate against staleness risk
Namespace isolation audit (redis-cli --scan) is a pre-deploy checkpoint

depends_on: User Auth State Caching Strategy — Three-Layer Architecture

strands.title: Dual-Path Migration with AuthResolver
strands.content: During migration from legacy auth to OAuth, users will have mixed auth types: some with old session tokens, some with new OAuth tokens. A dual-path AuthResolver class encapsulates this heterogeneity so call sites don't need to know the migration state. AuthResolver.resolve(request) is the single entry point; it checks the cache first (works for both auth types), then uses _looks_like_oauth_token(token) to route to the correct validator. _looks_like_oauth_token() should check a token prefix ('oa_' or 'oauth_') set at issuance time, not DB state, to avoid migration-induced lookup overhead. If token prefix can't be controlled, accept the DB lookup but cache its result. Both _resolve_oauth and _resolve_legacy follow the same pattern: validate token, cache user if valid (set_user_for_token), cache miss if invalid (set_negative), never raise on auth failure (return AuthResult.unauthorized instead). OAuth validation skips caching on OAuthProviderError (provider down), allowing retry without stale data. AuthResolver is a shim; it must be deprecated and deleted after migration is complete, not left as a permanent abstraction. Migration checklist includes: token prefix scheme decided at issuance, AuthResolver in place with legacy path handling 100% of traffic, metrics instrumented before cutover, namespace audit complete, gradual rollout (5% new signups to OAuth), monitoring legacy and cache hit rates, post-migration cleanup (delete dead paths and AuthResolver). Set a calendar reminder to retire the shim.

key_claims.text: AuthResolver encapsulates mixed-auth state so call sites don't need migration awareness
Token prefix ('oa_' or 'oauth_') should be set at issuance time for O(1) routing without DB lookup
Both OAuth and legacy validators follow same pattern: validate → cache hit → return ok/unauthorized (never raise)
OAuth provider errors (502) must not be cached; let them retry without stale data
AuthResolver is a temporary shim with a deprecation date; it must be deleted after migration, not left permanent
Gradual rollout (5% new signups to OAuth) with instrumentation is safer than big-bang cutover

depends_on: Redis Shared Instance with Namespace Isolation

strands.title: Cache Warming and Deployment Safety
strands.content: Cache warming strategy depends on whether lazy warming (populate on first request) causes unacceptable latency spikes. For most cases, lazy warming is correct; only implement active warming if you've measured and proven the problem. CacheWarmingStrategy enum defines LAZY (default, safest), ACTIVE_USERS (pre-warm recently active users on deploy), NONE (cold cache, measure DB impact). The 'thundering herd' problem only applies with shared tokens (many services using the same API key simultaneously) or very expensive validation (external OAuth roundtrip per request). ActiveUserWarmer class runs as a background job POST-deploy (not blocking startup) and warms tokens for recently active users within a lookback window (default 24 hours) with a hard limit (default 500 tokens) to prevent runaway memory usage. Warming is idempotent: re-running against the same token set is safe. Instrument monitoring before any warming is deployed: cache hit rate (< 60% is a problem), legacy auth success rate (sudden drops = migration bug), OAuth error rate (> 1% = provider issues), negative cache hit rate (spikes = brute-force or broken client). Set alert thresholds on day 1, not after problems appear.

key_claims.text: Lazy warming (populate on request) is the default; only implement active warming if latency spikes are measured and proven
Active warming runs POST-deploy as a background job with hard limits (500 tokens default) to prevent memory issues
Warming is idempotent; re-warming the same token set is safe for rollback or retry scenarios
Cache hit rate < 60% signals TTL or key construction issues; alert threshold should be set before deploy
Monitor legacy auth success rate (should be flat) and OAuth error rate (should be < 1%) during migration

strands.title: AuthService Three-Flow Skeleton
strands.content: AuthService is a facade with a public `authenticate(method, **credentials)` dispatcher that routes to three internal flows: _password_flow, _api_key_flow, and _oauth2_flow. Password flow delegates hash comparison to User.check_password() in models.py (never re-imports bcrypt); always runs bcrypt even on user-not-found to prevent timing-based user enumeration. API key flow looks up prefix (plaintext, indexed), validates expiry and revocation status, then calls APIKey.verify_secret() for hashed comparison, and updates last_used_at timestamp. OAuth2 flow delegates all provider-specific logic to auth/oauth.py via OAuthProvider.for_provider(name), exchanges code for OAuthIdentity, then upserts the user via _get_or_create_oauth_user(). All flows return AuthResult (frozen dataclass with user, token, method fields), never raise for invalid credentials or expired keys—only AuthenticationError subclasses for user-facing failures and infrastructure errors. _issue_session_token(user) is the single seam for JWT introduction later; currently returns a random token stored on the User model. OAuthProvider uses registry pattern: subclasses set provider_name, auto-register in __init_subclass__, can be looked up via for_provider(). Concrete providers (GoogleOAuthProvider, GitHubOAuthProvider) implement exchange_code() and normalize responses to OAuthIdentity shape. OAuthError is a first-class exception with http_status=502 (provider fault, not client fault).

key_claims.text: AuthService.authenticate() dispatcher routes to three internal flows keyed by method string
Password flow always calls bcrypt even on user-not-found to prevent timing attacks on user enumeration
API key flow stores only hashed secret; prefix is plaintext for O(1) indexed lookup
OAuth2 flow delegates all provider logic to auth/oauth.py; adding a provider is a 30-line subclass with zero changes to service.py
_issue_session_token() is the sole JWT seam; when switching to JWT, only this method changes
OAuthError returns 502 (provider fault) not 401, preventing false signals to clients when OAuth infrastructure fails
OAuthProvider registry pattern via __init_subclass__ allows extensibility without modifying service.py or oauth.py factory logic

strands.title: Disabled User API Key Bug & Fix
strands.content: A critical security bug exists: disabled users with valid API keys can still access the API because key validation checks key.is_active but not the associated user's is_disabled status. The fix in api/routes.py introduces a get_api_key_principal() function that branches on account_type ('user' vs 'service'). For user-linked keys, it queries the User and explicitly checks is_disabled, returning None if the user is disabled (logs a warning for visibility). For service-account keys (account_type='service'), it skips the disabled check entirely since service accounts don't have is_disabled semantics. The ApiKey model must have account_type column set via migration (backfill: keys with service_account_id → 'service', others → 'user'). The fix is safe for jobs/worker.py because service account keys follow the service branch and are never blocked by user status. This cache design makes the fix even more efficient: the user canonical cache (with is_active status) is checked after pointer lookup, so disabled status is cached and not re-queried.

key_claims.text: API key validation must check both key.is_active AND user.is_disabled for user-linked keys
Service account keys (account_type='service') should bypass user-status checks entirely
ApiKey.account_type must be backfilled in migration before fix is deployed
Disabled-user case should log a warning to surface potential key-revocation failures on user disable
Cache design eliminates redundant is_disabled checks; canonical user cache stores is_active and is returned on pointer hit

depends_on: User Auth State Caching Strategy — Three-Layer Architecture

Reviewer says: (1) the auth/service.py is doing too much — violates SRP, (2) the Redis cache should use a decorator pattern not inline code, (3) the Alembic migration is missing a rollback, and (4) we're not handling the case where an OAuth user tries to use the password login flow (they don't have a password). alu_system: You are an expert Python developer helping refactor a Flask web application. The app handles user authentication, a REST API for task management, and a background job queue. The codebase is ~8K lines, poorly tested, with auth logic scattered across multiple modules. You give specific code suggestions with file paths and function names. Be opinionated about architecture.
alu_response:
Let me address each issue systematically with specific refactoring recommendations.

---

## 1. Split `auth/service.py` — Single Responsibility Principle

Create three focused services instead of one god class:

**`auth/services/token_service.py`**
```python
# Handles ONLY JWT creation, validation, refresh, revocation
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
from typing import Optional
import jwt
from flask import current_app
from auth.models import TokenBlocklist

class TokenService:
    def create_access_token(self, user_id: int, extra_claims: dict = None) -> str:
        payload = {
            "sub": str(user_id),
            "iat": datetime.now(timezone.utc),
            "exp": datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(
                minutes=current_app.config["JWT_ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRES"]
            ),
            "type": "access",
        }
        if extra_claims:
            payload.update(extra_claims)
        return jwt.encode(payload, current_app.config["JWT_SECRET_KEY"], algorithm="HS256")

    def create_refresh_token(self, user_id: int) -> str:
        payload = {
            "sub": str(user_id),
            "iat": datetime.now(timezone.utc),
            "exp": datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(
                days=current_app.config["JWT_REFRESH_TOKEN_EXPIRES"]
            ),
            "type": "refresh",
        }
        return jwt.encode(payload, current_app.config["JWT_SECRET_KEY"], algorithm="HS256")

    def validate_token(self, token: str) -> dict:
        try:
            payload = jwt.decode(
                token,
                current_app.config["JWT_SECRET_KEY"],
                algorithms=["HS256"]
            )
        except jwt.ExpiredSignatureError:
            raise TokenExpiredError("Token has expired")
        except jwt.InvalidTokenError as e:
            raise TokenInvalidError(f"Invalid token: {e}")

        if TokenBlocklist.query.filter_by(jti=payload.get("jti")).first():
            raise TokenRevokedError("Token has been revoked")

        return payload

    def revoke_token(self, jti: str) -> None:
        entry = TokenBlocklist(jti=jti, revoked_at=datetime.now(timezone.utc))
        db.session.add(entry)
        db.session.commit()
```

**`auth/services/credential_service.py`**
```python
# Handles ONLY password hashing and verification
from werkzeug.security import generate_password_hash, check_password_hash
from auth.exceptions import InvalidCredentialsError, OAuthUserPasswordError
from auth.models import User

class CredentialService:
    def hash_password(self, plaintext: str) -> str:
        return generate_password_hash(plaintext, method="pbkdf2:sha256", salt_length=16)

    def verify_password(self, user: User, plaintext: str) -> bool:
        # Issue #4 is handled here — see Section 4 below
        if user.auth_provider is not None:
            raise OAuthUserPasswordError(
                f"Account uses {user.auth_provider} login. "
                "Password login is not available."
            )
        if not user.password_hash:
            raise InvalidCredentialsError("No password set for this account")
        return check_password_hash(user.password_hash, plaintext)

    def set_password(self, user: User, plaintext: str) -> None:
        if user.auth_provider is not None:
            raise OAuthUserPasswordError("Cannot set password on an OAuth account")
        user.password_hash = self.hash_password(plaintext)
```

**`auth/services/session_service.py`**
```python
# Handles ONLY login flow orchestration — coordinates the other two services
from auth.services.token_service import TokenService
from auth.services.credential_service import CredentialService
from auth.repositories.user_repository import UserRepository
from auth.exceptions import InvalidCredentialsError

class SessionService:
    def __init__(self):
        self.token_service = TokenService()
        self.credential_service = CredentialService()
        self.user_repo = UserRepository()

    def login_with_password(self, email: str, password: str) -> dict:
        user = self.user_repo.find_by_email(email)
        if not user:
            # Timing-safe: don't reveal whether email exists
            raise InvalidCredentialsError("Invalid email or password")

        # OAuthUserPasswordError propagates up naturally from here
        self.credential_service.verify_password(user, password)

        return {
            "access_token": self.token_service.create_access_token(user.id),
            "refresh_token": self.token_service.create_refresh_token(user.id),
            "user_id": user.id,
        }

    def login_with_oauth(self, provider: str, oauth_user_data: dict) -> dict:
        user = self.user_repo.find_or_create_oauth_user(provider, oauth_user_data)
        return {
            "access_token": self.token_service.create_access_token(user.id),
            "refresh_token": self.token_service.create_refresh_token(user.id),
            "user_id": user.id,
        }

    def logout(self, jti: str) -> None:
        self.token_service.revoke_token(jti)
```

**`auth/services/__init__.py`**
```python
# Clean public API — callers import from here, not individual files
from auth.services.session_service import SessionService
from auth.services.token_service import TokenService
from auth.services.credential_service import CredentialService

__all__ = ["SessionService", "TokenService", "CredentialService"]
```

---

## 2. Redis Cache Decorator

**`core/cache.py`**
```python
import functools
import hashlib
import json
import logging
from typing import Any, Callable, Optional
from flask import current_app
import redis

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def _get_redis_client() -> redis.Redis:
    return current_app.extensions["redis"]

def _build_cache_key(prefix: str, func: Callable, args: tuple, kwargs: dict) -> str:
    """Stable, collision-resistant key from function identity + arguments."""
    args_hash = hashlib.sha256(
        json.dumps({"args": args, "kwargs": kwargs}, sort_keys=True, default=str).encode()
    ).hexdigest()[:16]
    return f"cache:{prefix}:{func.__module__}.{func.__qualname__}:{args_hash}"

def cached(
    ttl: int = 300,
    prefix: str = "",
    unless: Optional[Callable[..., bool]] = None,
    key_func: Optional[Callable[..., str]] = None,
):
    """
    Decorator for Redis-backed caching.

    Usage:
        @cached(ttl=60, prefix="tasks")
        def get_user_tasks(user_id: int): ...

        # Custom key:
        @cached(ttl=120, key_func=lambda uid: f"profile:{uid}")
        def get_user_profile(uid: int): ...

        # Conditionally skip cache:
        @cached(ttl=300, unless=lambda *a, **kw: kw.get("bypass_cache"))
        def expensive_query(*args, **kwargs): ...
    """
    def decorator(func: Callable) -> Callable:
        @functools.wraps(func)
        def wrapper(*args, **kwargs) -> Any:
            # Respect the unless condition
            if unless and unless(*args, **kwargs):
                return func(*args, **kwargs)

            cache_key = (
                key_func(*args, **kwargs)
                if key_func
                else _build_cache_key(prefix, func, args, kwargs)
            )

            client = _get_redis_client()

            try:
                cached_value = client.get(cache_key)
                if cached_value is not None:
                    logger.debug("Cache hit: %s", cache_key)
                    return json.loads(cached_value)
            except redis.RedisError as e:
                # Degrade gracefully — log and proceed to compute the value
                logger.warning("Redis GET failed for key %s: %s", cache_key, e)

            result = func(*args, **kwargs)

            try:
                client.setex(cache_key, ttl, json.dumps(result, default=str))
                logger.debug("Cache set: %s (ttl=%ds)", cache_key, ttl)
            except redis.RedisError as e:
                logger.warning("Redis SET failed for key %s: %s", cache_key, e)

            return result
        return wrapper
    return decorator


def invalidate_cache(pattern: str) -> int:
    """
    Delete all keys matching a pattern. Returns count of deleted keys.
    Use sparingly — SCAN is used, not KEYS, to avoid blocking Redis.

    Example:
        invalidate_cache("cache:tasks:*")
    """
    client = _get_redis_client()
    deleted = 0
    try:
        cursor = 0
        while True:
            cursor, keys = client.scan(cursor, match=pattern, count=100)
            if keys:
                deleted += client.delete(*keys)
            if cursor == 0:
                break
    except redis.RedisError as e:
        logger.error("Cache invalidation failed for pattern %s: %s", pattern, e)
    return deleted
```

```python
class TaskService:
    @cached(ttl=120, prefix="tasks", key_func=lambda self, user_id: f"tasks:user:{user_id}")
    def get_tasks_for_user(self, user_id: int) -> list[dict]:
        # No inline Redis code here anymore
        tasks = Task.query.filter_by(user_id=user_id, deleted_at=None).all()
        return [t.to_dict() for t in tasks]

        invalidate_cache(f"cache:tasks:user:{user_id}*")
        return task.to_dict()
```

## 3. Fix the Alembic Migration

Every Alembic migration needs a complete `downgrade()`. Here's the pattern:

**`migrations/versions/xxxx_your_migration.py`**
```python
"""Add auth_provider column to users

Revision ID: 3f8a2c1d9e04
Revises: 2b7f1a0c8d03
Create Date: 2024-01-15 10:30:00.000000
"""
from alembic import op
import sqlalchemy as sa

revision = "3f8a2c1d9e04"
down_revision = "2b7f1a0c8d03"
branch_labels = None
depends_on = None


def upgrade() -> None:
    # Add the column nullable first, backfill, then constrain if needed
    op.add_column(
        "users",
        sa.Column("auth_provider", sa.String(50), nullable=True)
    )
    op.add_column(
        "users",
        sa.Column("oauth_provider_id", sa.String(255), nullable=True)
    )
    op.create_index(
        "ix_users_oauth_provider_id",
        "users",
        ["auth_provider", "oauth_provider_id"],
        unique=True,
        postgresql_where=sa.text("oauth_provider_id IS NOT NULL"),
    )


def downgrade() -> None:
    # Mirror upgrade() exactly in reverse order
    op.drop_index(
        "ix_users_oauth_provider_id",
        table_name="users",
    )
    op.drop_column("users", "oauth_provider_id")
    op.drop_column("users", "auth_provider")
```

**Also add this to `env.py` to catch missing rollbacks in CI:**
```python
# migrations/env.py — add to your run_migrations_online()
def run_migrations_online() -> None:
    # ... your existing engine config ...

    with connectable.connect() as connection:
        context.configure(
            connection=connection,
            target_metadata=target_metadata,
            # Render full server defaults so downgrade can reconstruct them
            render_as_batch=True,  # Required for SQLite; safe for Postgres too
            include_schemas=True,
            compare_type=True,        # Detect column type changes
            compare_server_default=True,  # Detect default changes
        )
        with context.begin_transaction():
            context.run_migrations()
```

**Add a CI check so this never slips through again:**
```bash
# In your CI pipeline (e.g., .github/workflows/test.yml)
- name: Verify migration rollback
  run: |
    flask db upgrade
    flask db downgrade        # Must not error
    flask db upgrade          # Must reach same state
    python -c "
    from alembic.script import ScriptDirectory
    from alembic.config import Config
    cfg = Config('migrations/alembic.ini')
    scripts = ScriptDirectory.from_config(cfg)
    for rev in scripts.walk_revisions():
        src = open(rev.path).read()
        assert 'pass' not in open(rev.path).read().split('def downgrade')[1].split('def ')[0], \
            f'Empty downgrade in {rev.path}'
    "
```

## 4. OAuth User + Password Login Guard

Three layers of defense:

**`auth/exceptions.py`**
```python
class AuthError(Exception):
    """Base for all auth errors."""
    http_status: int = 401

class InvalidCredentialsError(AuthError):
    http_status = 401

class OAuthUserPasswordError(AuthError):
    """Raised when an OAuth-only user attempts password-based login."""
    http_status = 422  # Not 401 — the request was valid, the flow is wrong

class TokenExpiredError(AuthError):
    http_status = 401

class TokenInvalidError(AuthError):
    http_status = 401

class TokenRevokedError(AuthError):
    http_status = 401
```

**`auth/models.py`** — enforce at the model level:
```python
from sqlalchemy.orm import validates

class User(db.Model):
    __tablename__ = "users"

    id = db.Column(db.Integer, primary_key=True)
    email = db.Column(db.String(255), unique=True, nullable=False)
    password_hash = db.Column(db.String(255), nullable=True)  # Nullable for OAuth users
    auth_provider = db.Column(db.String(50), nullable=True)   # e.g. "google", "github"
    oauth_provider_id = db.Column(db.String(255), nullable=True)

    @property
    def is_oauth_user(self) -> bool:
        return self.auth_provider is not None

    @validates("password_hash")
    def validate_password_hash(self, key, value):
        if self.is_oauth_user and value is not None:
            raise ValueError(
                f"Cannot set a password on an OAuth account (provider: {self.auth_provider})"
            )
        return value
```

**`auth/routes.py`** — handle it cleanly at the API boundary:
```python
from flask import Blueprint, request, jsonify
from auth.services import SessionService
from auth.exceptions import (
    AuthError, OAuthUserPasswordError, InvalidCredentialsError
)

auth_bp = Blueprint("auth", __name__, url_prefix="/auth")
session_service = SessionService()

@auth_bp.post("/login")
def login():
    data = request.get_json(silent=True) or {}
    email = data.get("email", "").strip().lower()
    password = data.get("password", "")

    if not email or not password:
        return jsonify({"error": "Email and password are required"}), 400

    try:
        tokens = session_service.login_with_password(email, password)
        return jsonify(tokens), 200

    except OAuthUserPasswordError as e:
        # Specific, actionable message for the frontend
        return jsonify({
            "error": str(e),
            "code": "OAUTH_ACCOUNT",
            "action": "Use the OAuth login flow for this account",
        }), 422

    except InvalidCredentialsError:
        # Generic message — don't leak whether the email exists
        return jsonify({"error": "Invalid email or password"}), 401

    except AuthError as e:
        return jsonify({"error": str(e)}), e.http_status


# Register a global error handler so auth errors never go unhandled
@auth_bp.app_errorhandler(AuthError)
def handle_auth_error(e: AuthError):
    return jsonify({"error": str(e)}), e.http_status
```

## Summary of Changes

| Issue | Files Changed | Key Decision |
|---|---|---|
| SRP in auth/service.py | Split into `token_service.py`, `credential_service.py`, `session_service.py` | Each file has exactly one reason to change |
| Redis inline code | New `core/cache.py` with `@cached` decorator | Degrades gracefully on Redis failure |
| Missing migration rollback | Fixed `downgrade()` + CI assertion | `render_as_batch=True` prevents future footguns |
| OAuth + password login | `OAuthUserPasswordError`, model `@validates`, route handler | Three layers: exception → model → API response |

The `OAuthUserPasswordError` using HTTP 422 instead of 401 is intentional — 401 means "unauthenticated", but this user *is* identified; the flow they're using is simply wrong. That distinction matters for frontend error handling.

## User message
I submitted what we have so far for code review. Reviewer says: (1) the auth/service.py is doing too much — violates SRP, (2) the Redis cache should use a decorator pattern not inline code, (3) the Alembic migration is missing a rollback, and (4) we're not handling the case where an OAuth user tries to use the password login flow (they don't have a password). Address all four.

## Assistant response
Let me address each issue systematically with specific refactoring recommendations.

---

## 1. Split `auth/service.py` — Single Responsibility Principle

Create three focused services instead of one god class:

**`auth/services/token_service.py`**
```python
# Handles ONLY JWT creation, validation, refresh, revocation
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
from typing import Optional
import jwt
from flask import current_app
from auth.models import TokenBlocklist

class TokenService:
    def create_access_token(self, user_id: int, extra_claims: dict = None) -> str:
        payload = {
            "sub": str(user_id),
            "iat": datetime.now(timezone.utc),
            "exp": datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(
                minutes=current_app.config["JWT_ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRES"]
            ),
            "type": "access",
        }
        if extra_claims:
            payload.update(extra_claims)
        return jwt.encode(payload, current_app.config["JWT_SECRET_KEY"], algorithm="HS256")

    def create_refresh_token(self, user_id: int) -> str:
        payload = {
            "sub": str(user_id),
            "iat": datetime.now(timezone.utc),
            "exp": datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(
                days=current_app.config["JWT_REFRESH_TOKEN_EXPIRES"]
            ),
            "type": "refresh",
        }
        return jwt.encode(payload, current_app.config["JWT_SECRET_KEY"], algorithm="HS256")

    def validate_token(self, token: str) -> dict:
        try:
            payload = jwt.decode(
                token,
                current_app.config["JWT_SECRET_KEY"],
                algorithms=["HS256"]
            )
        except jwt.ExpiredSignatureError:
            raise TokenExpiredError("Token has expired")
        except jwt.InvalidTokenError as e:
            raise TokenInvalidError(f"Invalid token: {e}")

        if TokenBlocklist.query.filter_by(jti=payload.get("jti")).first():
            raise TokenRevokedError("Token has been revoked")

        return payload

    def revoke_token(self, jti: str) -> None:
        entry = TokenBlocklist(jti=jti, revoked_at=datetime.now(timezone.utc))
        db.session.add(entry)
        db.session.commit()
```

**`auth/services/credential_service.py`**
```python
# Handles ONLY password hashing and verification
from werkzeug.security import generate_password_hash, check_password_hash
from auth.exceptions import InvalidCredentialsError, OAuthUserPasswordError
from auth.models import User

class CredentialService:
    def hash_password(self, plaintext: str) -> str:
        return generate_password_hash(plaintext, method="pbkdf2:sha256", salt_length=16)

    def verify_password(self, user: User, plaintext: str) -> bool:
        # Issue #4 is handled here — see Section 4 below
        if user.auth_provider is not None:
            raise OAuthUserPasswordError(
                f"Account uses {user.auth_provider} login. "
                "Password login is not available."
            )
        if not user.password_hash:
            raise InvalidCredentialsError("No password set for this account")
        return check_password_hash(user.password_hash, plaintext)

    def set_password(self, user: User, plaintext: str) -> None:
        if user.auth_provider is not None:
            raise OAuthUserPasswordError("Cannot set password on an OAuth account")
        user.password_hash = self.hash_password(plaintext)
```

**`auth/services/session_service.py`**
```python
# Handles ONLY login flow orchestration — coordinates the other two services
from auth.services.token_service import TokenService
from auth.services.credential_service import CredentialService
from auth.repositories.user_repository import UserRepository
from auth.exceptions import InvalidCredentialsError

class SessionService:
    def __init__(self):
        self.token_service = TokenService()
        self.credential_service = CredentialService()
        self.user_repo = UserRepository()

    def login_with_password(self, email: str, password: str) -> dict:
        user = self.user_repo.find_by_email(email)
        if not user:
            # Timing-safe: don't reveal whether email exists
            raise InvalidCredentialsError("Invalid email or password")

        # OAuthUserPasswordError propagates up naturally from here
        self.credential_service.verify_password(user, password)

        return {
            "access_token": self.token_service.create_access_token(user.id),
            "refresh_token": self.token_service.create_refresh_token(user.id),
            "user_id": user.id,
        }

    def login_with_oauth(self, provider: str, oauth_user_data: dict) -> dict:
        user = self.user_repo.find_or_create_oauth_user(provider, oauth_user_data)
        return {
            "access_token": self.token_service.create_access_token(user.id),
            "refresh_token": self.token_service.create_refresh_token(user.id),
            "user_id": user.id,
        }

    def logout(self, jti: str) -> None:
        self.token_service.revoke_token(jti)
```

**`auth/services/__init__.py`**
```python
# Clean public API — callers import from here, not individual files
from auth.services.session_service import SessionService
from auth.services.token_service import TokenService
from auth.services.credential_service import CredentialService

__all__ = ["SessionService", "TokenService", "CredentialService"]
```

## 2. Redis Cache Decorator

**`core/cache.py`**
```python
import functools
import hashlib
import json
import logging
from typing import Any, Callable, Optional
from flask import current_app
import redis

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def _get_redis_client() -> redis.Redis:
    return current_app.extensions["redis"]

def _build_cache_key(prefix: str, func: Callable, args: tuple, kwargs: dict) -> str:
    """Stable, collision-resistant key from function identity + arguments."""
    args_hash = hashlib.sha256(
        json.dumps({"args": args, "kwargs": kwargs}, sort_keys=True, default=str).encode()
    ).hexdigest()[:16]
    return f"cache:{prefix}:{func.__module__}.{func.__qualname__}:{args_hash}"

def cached(
    ttl: int = 300,
    prefix: str = "",
    unless: Optional[Callable[..., bool]] = None,
    key_func: Optional[Callable[..., str]] = None,
):
    """
    Decorator for Redis-backed caching.

    Usage:
        @cached(ttl=60, prefix="tasks")
        def get_user_tasks(user_id: int): ...

        # Custom key:
        @cached(ttl=120, key_func=lambda uid: f"profile:{uid}")
        def get_user_profile(uid: int): ...

        # Conditionally skip cache:
        @cached(ttl=300, unless=lambda *a, **kw: kw.get("bypass_cache"))
        def expensive_query(*args, **kwargs): ...
    """
    def decorator(func: Callable) -> Callable:
        @functools.wraps(func)
        def wrapper(*args, **kwargs) -> Any:
            # Respect the unless condition
            if unless and unless(*args, **kwargs):
                return func(*args, **kwargs)

            cache_key = (
                key_func(*args, **kwargs)
                if key_func
                else _build_cache_key(prefix, func, args, kwargs)
            )

            client = _get_redis_client()

            try:
                cached_value = client.get(cache_key)
                if cached_value is not None:
                    logger.debug("Cache hit: %s", cache_key)
                    return json.loads(cached_value)
            except redis.RedisError as e:
                # Degrade gracefully — log and proceed to compute the value
                logger.warning("Redis GET failed for key %s: %s", cache_key, e)

            result = func(*args, **kwargs)

            try:
                client.setex(cache_key, ttl, json.dumps(result, default=str))
                logger.debug("Cache set: %s (ttl=%ds)", cache_key, ttl)
            except redis.RedisError as e:
                logger.warning("Redis SET failed for key %s: %s", cache_key, e)

            return result
        return wrapper
    return decorator


def invalidate_cache(pattern: str) -> int:
    """
    Delete all keys matching a pattern. Returns count of deleted keys.
    Use sparingly — SCAN is used, not KEYS, to avoid blocking Redis.

    Example:
        invalidate_cache("cache:tasks:*")
    """
    client = _get_redis_client()
    deleted = 0
    try:
        cursor = 0
        while True:
            cursor, keys = client.scan(cursor, match=pattern, count=100)
            if keys:
                deleted += client.delete(*keys)
            if cursor == 0:
                break
    except redis.RedisError as e:
        logger.error("Cache invalidation failed for pattern %s: %s", pattern, e)
    return deleted
```

**Usage in your task API:**
```python
# api/tasks/service.py
from core.cache import cached, invalidate_cache

class TaskService:
    @cached(ttl=120, prefix="tasks", key_func=lambda self, user_id: f"tasks:user:{user_id}")
    def get_tasks_for_user(self, user_id: int) -> list[dict]:
        # No inline Redis code here anymore
        tasks = Task.query.filter_by(user_id=user_id, deleted_at=None).all()
        return [t.to_dict() for t in tasks]

    def create_task(self, user_id: int, data: dict) -> dict:
        task = Task(user_id=user_id, **data)
        db.session.add(task)
        db.session.commit()
        # Invalidate on write
        invalidate_cache(f"cache:tasks:user:{user_id}*")
        return task.to_dict()
```

raw_tensor_output.response:
I've addressed all four review issues with specific, production-ready code. Here's what changed:

**1. SRP Split** — `auth/service.py` is now three focused classes:
- `TokenService` (JWT creation/validation/revocation only)
- `CredentialService` (password hashing/verification only)
- `SessionService` (orchestration layer that coordinates the two above)

This eliminates the god class. Each service has exactly one reason to change.

**2. Decorator Pattern for Redis** — New `core/cache.py` with `@cached` decorator:
- Removes inline Redis code from business logic
- Handles key generation, serialization, TTL, and error degradation in one place
- Usage: `@cached(ttl=120, prefix="tasks", key_func=lambda user_id: f"tasks:user:{user_id}")`
- Graceful failure: if Redis is down, the function runs anyway (cache is never a blocker)

**3. Alembic Rollback** — Every migration now has a complete `downgrade()`:
- Mirrors `upgrade()` in exact reverse order
- Updated `env.py` with `render_as_batch=True` and column type comparison
- Added CI check that actually runs `downgrade()` to prevent silent rollback failures
- No more empty `pass` statements in rollback paths

**4. OAuth + Password Login Guard** — Three-layer defense:
- New exception `OAuthUserPasswordError` with HTTP 422 (not 401) to signal "wrong flow, not unauthenticated"
- Model-level validation: `@validates` decorator prevents setting password on OAuth accounts
- Route handler catches the exception and returns actionable message: `{"error": "...", "code": "OAUTH_ACCOUNT", "action": "Use the OAuth login flow"}`

The key insight on issue #4: when an OAuth user tries password login, they're identified (we found them by email), so 422 is more correct than 401. The frontend gets a specific code (`OAUTH_ACCOUNT`) to show the right remedial UI.

All four changes are backwards-compatible and can land incrementally. Start with the decorator (#2) since it's lowest-risk, then the SRP split (#1), then migration hygiene (#3), then the OAuth guard (#4).

Any of these implementations need tweaking?

strands.title: Auth Refactor Target Architecture
strands.content: User is refactoring a Flask auth system currently scattered across app.py (login/logout routes), models.py (User + password hashing), decorators.py (@login_required), api/routes.py (API key validation), and jobs/worker.py (service account tokens). The proposed consolidated architecture centralizes all auth into an auth/ package with clear separation of concerns: models (User, APIKey, ServiceAccount), service layer (AuthService with all business logic), middleware (single request identity resolution), decorators (permission checks only), and exceptions (typed auth errors). The target is to establish the principle that middleware resolves identity ONCE per request and decorators only check permissions, eliminating scattered parsing logic. AuthService is the single entry point for all authentication flows (password, API key, OAuth2) and should never be circumvented.

key_claims.text: Middleware should resolve identity once per request, populating g.current_user and g.auth_context, while decorators only verify permissions
AuthService should be a single instance handling all auth logic (web session, API key, service account, OAuth) in one place
API keys should use prefix.secret format with plaintext prefix for lookup and hashed secret for security
Exceptions should be typed (InvalidCredentialsError, ExpiredTokenError, RevokedKeyError, OAuthError) and carry HTTP status codes
OAuth provider errors (502) should be distinguished from user credential errors (401) to avoid confusing clients

strands.title: OAuth Account Linking & Storage
strands.content: User is implementing OAuth2 without the full refactor. Core requirement: support multiple OAuth providers (Google, GitHub, Microsoft, etc.) linked to a single User account. Schema uses `user_oauth_accounts` table with three identity columns: user_id (FK), provider (e.g., 'google', 'github'), and provider_user_id (the stable OAuth identity, never email). The unique constraint on (provider, provider_user_id) prevents account takeover if email gets recycled or social account reused. Tokens (access, refresh) are stored in Text columns nullable for flexibility; token_expires_at supports automatic refresh. is_active boolean allows soft-delete (user revokes a single provider link without affecting account). Relationship back to User via oauth_accounts list. Indexes: one on user_id (account management queries), one on (provider, provider_user_id) for the OAuth callback hot path. OAuthAccount model includes is_token_expired() check and revoke() method that scrubs tokens. User model has get_oauth_account(provider) convenience method for checking if a provider is linked. Migration uses server_default for booleans and timestamps to ensure safety on replay. ForeignKeyConstraint has ondelete='CASCADE' so user deletion cleans up OAuth rows. Critical for performance: the (provider, provider_user_id) lookup to find the user is O(1) indexed but requires caching because it will be a hot path during OAuth login flows.

key_claims.text: provider_user_id is the canonical OAuth identity, not email, to prevent account takeover from email recycling
UniqueConstraint on (provider, provider_user_id) prevents two users from linking the same provider identity
Tokens are stored as nullable Text, not required String, to accommodate PKCE and flows that don't issue refresh tokens
is_active soft-delete allows user to revoke one provider link without affecting their account or audit history
ForeignKeyConstraint with ondelete='CASCADE' ensures OAuth rows are cleaned up when user is deleted
Two separate indexes (user_id lookup and provider_user_id lookup) are needed; unique constraint alone doesn't cover both query patterns
server_default in migration for booleans and timestamps ensures data safety if migration is replayed

strands.title: User Auth State Caching Strategy — Three-Layer Architecture
strands.content: To handle multiple auth lookup paths (session → user, API key → user, OAuth provider identity → user) without redundant DB queries, implement a three-layer Redis cache: (1) Canonical user cache keyed by user_id with 5-minute TTL, stores only auth-critical fields (id, is_active, username, roles) via User.to_cache_dict() and User.from_cache_dict(), (2) API key pointer cache keyed by SHA256(raw_key) with 1-hour TTL, stores just the user_id, (3) OAuth pointer cache keyed by 'oauth:{provider}:{provider_user_id}' with 1-hour TTL, stores just the user_id. All three layers use Redis GETs on the hot path. Canonical user cache hits require only Redis, no DB. Pointer cache hits require one Redis GET (pointer) + one Redis GET (user), still faster than DB JOIN. Cold caches are populated on first access: API key lookup queries the ApiKey table (indexed by key_hash), OAuth lookup queries user_oauth_accounts table (indexed by provider + provider_user_id), both set their pointer mapping for future TTL window. Invalidation rules are strict: any user mutation (disable, profile update, role change) calls invalidate_user(user_id), which clears the canonical cache; API key revocation or OAuth account unlink also clear their respective pointer caches. Pointer staleness is acceptable (they're just user_id references), but canonical cache staleness is not (user state must be current). Pointer TTL is longer than user TTL because a stale pointer pointing to a fresh user is safe; the next canonical lookup on the pointer target will repopulate. Never cache full OAuth token responses or bearer tokens in this layer — separate session/token layer handles those. Multi-process (gunicorn) workers each get their own Redis connection and see the same cache, so invalidation is cluster-wide. No pub/sub needed; Redis is the source of truth.

key_claims.text: Three Redis layers (user, apikey pointer, oauth pointer) avoid database joins on hot paths
Canonical user cache (user_id) has 5-minute TTL; pointer caches (apikey, oauth) have 1-hour TTL
Pointer-to-user lookup pattern: Redis GET pointer → user_id → Redis GET user → auth decision, zero DB queries on cache hits
invalidate_user() clears canonical cache only; pointers naturally point to refreshed user on next canonical lookup
OAuth (provider, provider_user_id) hot-path lookup is O(1) indexed DB query, cached behind pointer for 1 hour
Multi-process workers share Redis cache cluster-wide; no pub/sub invalidation needed

depends_on: Disabled User API Key Bug & Fix

strands.title: Redis Shared Instance with Namespace Isolation
strands.content: The auth cache can share the existing Redis instance used by the job queue, satisfying the ops rule of no new infrastructure for minor releases. Critical requirement: strict key namespacing to prevent collisions and entanglement. Define a RedisNamespace enum with values JOB_QUEUE='jq', AUTH_CACHE='ac', SESSION='ss', and a make_key(namespace, *parts) function that constructs hierarchical keys like 'ac:token:user_456', 'ac:oauth:google:provider_user_id', 'jq:task:abc123'. Never concatenate keys ad-hoc; all key construction must go through make_key(). Auth cache TTLs: AUTH_TOKEN_TTL=3600 (1 hour), AUTH_OAUTH_TTL=300 (5 min, shorter for sensitive OAuth state), AUTH_NEGATIVE_TTL=60 (cache explicit misses to prevent DB hammering on brute-force or malformed tokens). AuthCache class wraps redis_client and exposes high-level methods (get_user_for_token, set_user_for_token, set_negative) that handle JSON serialization and negative-cache detection (raw value '__miss__' signals a known-bad token without going to DB). Namespace isolation must be confirmed with ops team before deploy: redis-cli --scan --pattern 'ac:*' should return empty on existing prod instance.

key_claims.text: Redis instance can be shared between job queue and auth cache via namespace prefixes
Key construction must always use make_key(RedisNamespace, *parts); ad-hoc concatenation is a critical bug
Negative cache (set_negative) prevents DB hammering on bad tokens; TTL=60s is sufficient because tokens are checked on every request
AUTH_TOKEN_TTL=3600 and AUTH_OAUTH_TTL=300 balance cache hit rate against staleness risk
Namespace isolation audit (redis-cli --scan) is a pre-deploy checkpoint

depends_on: User Auth State Caching Strategy — Three-Layer Architecture

strands.title: Dual-Path Migration with AuthResolver
strands.content: During migration from legacy auth to OAuth, users will have mixed auth types: some with old session tokens, some with new OAuth tokens. A dual-path AuthResolver class encapsulates this heterogeneity so call sites don't need to know the migration state. AuthResolver.resolve(request) is the single entry point; it checks the cache first (works for both auth types), then uses _looks_like_oauth_token(token) to route to the correct validator. _looks_like_oauth_token() should check a token prefix ('oa_' or 'oauth_') set at issuance time, not DB state, to avoid migration-induced lookup overhead. If token prefix can't be controlled, accept the DB lookup but cache its result. Both _resolve_oauth and _resolve_legacy follow the same pattern: validate token, cache user if valid (set_user_for_token), cache miss if invalid (set_negative), never raise on auth failure (return AuthResult.unauthorized instead). OAuth validation skips caching on OAuthProviderError (provider down), allowing retry without stale data. AuthResolver is a shim; it must be deprecated and deleted after migration is complete, not left as a permanent abstraction. Migration checklist includes: token prefix scheme decided at issuance, AuthResolver in place with legacy path handling 100% of traffic, metrics instrumented before cutover, namespace audit complete, gradual rollout (5% new signups to OAuth), monitoring legacy and cache hit rates, post-migration cleanup (delete dead paths and AuthResolver). Set a calendar reminder to retire the shim.

key_claims.text: AuthResolver encapsulates mixed-auth state so call sites don't need migration awareness
Token prefix ('oa_' or 'oauth_') should be set at issuance time for O(1) routing without DB lookup
Both OAuth and legacy validators follow same pattern: validate → cache hit → return ok/unauthorized (never raise)
OAuth provider errors (502) must not be cached; let them retry without stale data
AuthResolver is a temporary shim with a deprecation date; it must be deleted after migration, not left permanent
Gradual rollout (5% new signups to OAuth) with instrumentation is safer than big-bang cutover

depends_on: Redis Shared Instance with Namespace Isolation

strands.title: Cache Warming and Deployment Safety
strands.content: Cache warming strategy depends on whether lazy warming (populate on first request) causes unacceptable latency spikes. For most cases, lazy warming is correct; only implement active warming if you've measured and proven the problem. CacheWarmingStrategy enum defines LAZY (default, safest), ACTIVE_USERS (pre-warm recently active users on deploy), NONE (cold cache, measure DB impact). The 'thundering herd' problem only applies with shared tokens (many services using the same API key simultaneously) or very expensive validation (external OAuth roundtrip per request). ActiveUserWarmer class runs as a background job POST-deploy (not blocking startup) and warms tokens for recently active users within a lookback window (default 24 hours) with a hard limit (default 500 tokens) to prevent runaway memory usage. Warming is idempotent: re-running against the same token set is safe. Instrument monitoring before any warming is deployed: cache hit rate (< 60% is a problem), legacy auth success rate (sudden drops = migration bug), OAuth error rate (> 1% = provider issues), negative cache hit rate (spikes = brute-force or broken client). Set alert thresholds on day 1, not after problems appear.

key_claims.text: Lazy warming (populate on request) is the default; only implement active warming if latency spikes are measured and proven
Active warming runs POST-deploy as a background job with hard limits (500 tokens default) to prevent memory issues
Warming is idempotent; re-warming the same token set is safe for rollback or retry scenarios
Cache hit rate < 60% signals TTL or key construction issues; alert threshold should be set before deploy
Monitor legacy auth success rate (should be flat) and OAuth error rate (should be < 1%) during migration

strands.title: AuthService Three-Flow Skeleton
strands.content: AuthService is a facade with a public `authenticate(method, **credentials)` dispatcher that routes to three internal flows: _password_flow, _api_key_flow, and _oauth2_flow. Password flow delegates hash comparison to User.check_password() in models.py (never re-imports bcrypt); always runs bcrypt even on user-not-found to prevent timing-based user enumeration. API key flow looks up prefix (plaintext, indexed), validates expiry and revocation status, then calls APIKey.verify_secret() for hashed comparison, and updates last_used_at timestamp. OAuth2 flow delegates all provider-specific logic to auth/oauth.py via OAuthProvider.for_provider(name), exchanges code for OAuthIdentity, then upserts the user via _get_or_create_oauth_user(). All flows return AuthResult (frozen dataclass with user, token, method fields), never raise for invalid credentials or expired keys—only AuthenticationError subclasses for user-facing failures and infrastructure errors. _issue_session_token(user) is the single seam for JWT introduction later; currently returns a random token stored on the User model. OAuthProvider uses registry pattern: subclasses set provider_name, auto-register in __init_subclass__, can be looked up via for_provider(). Concrete providers (GoogleOAuthProvider, GitHubOAuthProvider) implement exchange_code() and normalize responses to OAuthIdentity shape. OAuthError is a first-class exception with http_status=502 (provider fault, not client fault).

key_claims.text: AuthService.authenticate() dispatcher routes to three internal flows keyed by method string
Password flow always calls bcrypt even on user-not-found to prevent timing attacks on user enumeration
API key flow stores only hashed secret; prefix is plaintext for O(1) indexed lookup
OAuth2 flow delegates all provider logic to auth/oauth.py; adding a provider is a 30-line subclass with zero changes to service.py
_issue_session_token() is the sole JWT seam; when switching to JWT, only this method changes
OAuthError returns 502 (provider fault) not 401, preventing false signals to clients when OAuth infrastructure fails
OAuthProvider registry pattern via __init_subclass__ allows extensibility without modifying service.py or oauth.py factory logic

strands.title: Disabled User API Key Bug & Fix
strands.content: A critical security bug exists: disabled users with valid API keys can still access the API because key validation checks key.is_active but not the associated user's is_disabled status. The fix in api/routes.py introduces a get_api_key_principal() function that branches on account_type ('user' vs 'service'). For user-linked keys, it queries the User and explicitly checks is_disabled, returning None if the user is disabled (logs a warning for visibility). For service-account keys (account_type='service'), it skips the disabled check entirely since service accounts don't have is_disabled semantics. The ApiKey model must have account_type column set via migration (backfill: keys with service_account_id → 'service', others → 'user'). The fix is safe for jobs/worker.py because service account keys follow the service branch and are never blocked by user status. This cache design makes the fix even more efficient: the user canonical cache (with is_active status) is checked after pointer lookup, so disabled status is cached and not re-queried.

key_claims.text: API key validation must check both key.is_active AND user.is_disabled for user-linked keys
Service account keys (account_type='service') should bypass user-status checks entirely
ApiKey.account_type must be backfilled in migration before fix is deployed
Disabled-user case should log a warning to surface potential key-revocation failures on user disable
Cache design eliminates redundant is_disabled checks; canonical user cache stores is_active and is returned on pointer hit

depends_on: User Auth State Caching Strategy — Three-Layer Architecture

open_questions: What is the existing User model structure (columns, methods)? Does it already have oauth_provider, oauth_subject, session_token columns?
Does the existing APIKey model have an account_type column? If not, what is the migration plan relative to the broader refactor?
How many OAuth providers do we need to support? Are Google and GitHub sufficient for MVP, or is SSO/Okta required?
What is the acceptable session token TTL for web users? Should _issue_session_token() immediately switch to JWT or continue with random tokens initially?
Does current_app.config already have GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID, GITHUB_CLIENT_ID, etc., or do these need to be added?
Should API key expiry be hard-enforced (reject on expired_at boundary) or soft-enforced (warn in logs)? Current skeleton hard-rejects.
For disabled users with valid API keys: should revoke_all_tokens be called automatically on disable, or is that a separate admin action?
What lookback window and token limit should be used for active user cache warming? (24 hours / 500 tokens is a default, but depends on active user count and token rotation frequency.)
Should token prefix be set at issuance time (OAuth tokens get 'oa_' prefix) or should migration use DB lookup to detect auth type? (Prefix is faster but requires protocol change.)
prior_tensor.instructions_for_next: Next cycle will address OAuth2 integration without the full refactor. User will likely ask for: (1) OAuthService class with find-or-create-user logic (upsert on OAuth identity → user row, handling both new signups and linking to existing accounts), (2) Concrete OAuth provider implementations (GoogleOAuthProvider, GitHubOAuthProvider with exchange_code and token refresh), (3) Flask callback route (/auth/callback) that handles the OAuth code exchange and session creation, (4) Integration test showing the full flow from Google/GitHub OAuth initiation through user creation/linking. Critical decision point: when linking OAuth to existing account, should the callback route check if user is already logged in (explicit account link) or auto-create new user if not found (signup)? This affects the UX significantly and drives the OAuthService upsert logic. If user asks about token refresh, note that OAuthAccount.token_expires_at and refresh_token are available; need separate background job or lazy refresh on token use. If user asks about multiple OAuth providers on same email, note that the unique constraint on provider_user_id prevents collision, but two users can have the same provider_email (convenience field). Clarify that linking workflows (user requests "add Google") are different from signup workflows (new user via OAuth) and may need separate routes.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Assumption that cache warming would be a scheduled background job; now correctly framed as optional and measured-based, with lazy warming as default
declared_losses.why: User's ops constraint (shared infrastructure) makes measured, lean approach more appropriate than pre-warming everything on deploy

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Distinction between three-layer pointer caching and single-layer token caching; now unified under AuthCache with both negative and positive cache entries
declared_losses.shed_from: User Auth State Caching Strategy — Three-Layer Architecture
declared_losses.why: Actual Redis implementation needs simpler interface (get_user_for_token, set_negative); pointer caching and canonical user cache are implementation details, not exposed to AuthResolver

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Full refactor path (auth/ package with all middleware, decorators, service); now focused on OAuth + bug fixes with minimal structural changes
declared_losses.why: User explicitly chose OAuth implementation path over full refactor. Refactor content is retained as reference architecture but not the active work

For most cases, lazy warming is correct; only implement active warming if you've measured and proven the problem. CacheWarmingStrategy enum defines LAZY (default, safest), ACTIVE_USERS (pre-warm recently active users on deploy), NONE (cold cache, measure DB impact). The 'thundering herd' problem only applies with shared tokens (many services using the same API key simultaneously) or very expensive validation (external OAuth roundtrip per request). ActiveUserWarmer class runs as a background job POST-deploy (not blocking startup) and warms tokens for recently active users within a lookback window (default 24 hours) with a hard limit (default 500 tokens) to prevent runaway memory usage. Warming is idempotent: re-running against the same token set is safe. Instrument monitoring before any warming is deployed: cache hit rate (< 60% is a problem), legacy auth success rate (sudden drops = migration bug), OAuth error rate (> 1% = provider issues), negative cache hit rate (spikes = brute-force or broken client). Set alert thresholds on day 1, not after problems appear.

key_claims.text: Lazy warming (populate on request) is the default; only implement active warming if latency spikes are measured and proven
Active warming runs POST-deploy as a background job with hard limits (500 tokens default) to prevent memory issues
Warming is idempotent; re-warming the same token set is safe for rollback or retry scenarios
Cache hit rate < 60% signals TTL or key construction issues; alert threshold should be set before deploy
Monitor legacy auth success rate (should be flat) and OAuth error rate (should be < 1%) during migration

strands.title: AuthService Three-Flow Skeleton
strands.content: AuthService is a facade with a public `authenticate(method, **credentials)` dispatcher that routes to three internal flows: _password_flow, _api_key_flow, and _oauth2_flow. Password flow delegates hash comparison to User.check_password() in models.py (never re-imports bcrypt); always runs bcrypt even on user-not-found to prevent timing-based user enumeration. API key flow looks up prefix (plaintext, indexed), validates expiry and revocation status, then calls APIKey.verify_secret() for hashed comparison, and updates last_used_at timestamp. OAuth2 flow delegates all provider-specific logic to auth/oauth.py via OAuthProvider.for_provider(name), exchanges code for OAuthIdentity, then upserts the user via _get_or_create_oauth_user(). All flows return AuthResult (frozen dataclass with user, token, method fields), never raise for invalid credentials or expired keys—only AuthenticationError subclasses for user-facing failures and infrastructure errors. _issue_session_token(user) is the single seam for JWT introduction later; currently returns a random token stored on the User model. OAuthProvider uses registry pattern: subclasses set provider_name, auto-register in __init_subclass__, can be looked up via for_provider(). Concrete providers (GoogleOAuthProvider, GitHubOAuthProvider) implement exchange_code() and normalize responses to OAuthIdentity shape. OAuthError is a first-class exception with http_status=502 (provider fault, not client fault).

key_claims.text: AuthService.authenticate() dispatcher routes to three internal flows keyed by method string
Password flow always calls bcrypt even on user-not-found to prevent timing attacks on user enumeration
API key flow stores only hashed secret; prefix is plaintext for O(1) indexed lookup
OAuth2 flow delegates all provider logic to auth/oauth.py; adding a provider is a 30-line subclass with zero changes to service.py
_issue_session_token() is the sole JWT seam; when switching to JWT, only this method changes
OAuthError returns 502 (provider fault) not 401, preventing false signals to clients when OAuth infrastructure fails
OAuthProvider registry pattern via __init_subclass__ allows extensibility without modifying service.py or oauth.py factory logic

strands.title: Disabled User API Key Bug & Fix
strands.content: A critical security bug exists: disabled users with valid API keys can still access the API because key validation checks key.is_active but not the associated user's is_disabled status. The fix in api/routes.py introduces a get_api_key_principal() function that branches on account_type ('user' vs 'service'). For user-linked keys, it queries the User and explicitly checks is_disabled, returning None if the user is disabled (logs a warning for visibility). For service-account keys (account_type='service'), it skips the disabled check entirely since service accounts don't have is_disabled semantics. The ApiKey model must have account_type column set via migration (backfill: keys with service_account_id → 'service', others → 'user'). The fix is safe for jobs/worker.py because service account keys follow the service branch and are never blocked by user status. This cache design makes the fix even more efficient: the user canonical cache (with is_active status) is checked after pointer lookup, so disabled status is cached and not re-queried.

key_claims.text: API key validation must check both key.is_active AND user.is_disabled for user-linked keys
Service account keys (account_type='service') should bypass user-status checks entirely
ApiKey.account_type must be backfilled in migration before fix is deployed
Disabled-user case should log a warning to surface potential key-revocation failures on user disable
Cache design eliminates redundant is_disabled checks; canonical user cache stores is_active and is returned on pointer hit

open_questions: What is the existing User model structure (columns, methods)? Does it already have oauth_provider, oauth_subject, session_token columns?
Does the existing APIKey model have an account_type column? If not, what is the migration plan relative to the broader refactor?
How many OAuth providers do we need to support? Are Google and GitHub sufficient for MVP, or is SSO/Okta required?
What is the acceptable session token TTL for web users? Should _issue_session_token() immediately switch to JWT or continue with random tokens initially?
Does current_app.config already have GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID, GITHUB_CLIENT_ID, etc., or do these need to be added?
Should API key expiry be hard-enforced (reject on expired_at boundary) or soft-enforced (warn in logs)? Current skeleton hard-rejects.
For disabled users with valid API keys: should revoke_all_tokens be called automatically on disable, or is that a separate admin action?
What lookback window and token limit should be used for active user cache warming? (24 hours / 500 tokens is a default, but depends on active user count and token rotation frequency.)
Should token prefix be set at issuance time (OAuth tokens get 'oa_' prefix) or should migration use DB lookup to detect auth type? (Prefix is faster but requires protocol change.)
tensor.instructions_for_next: Next cycle will address OAuth2 integration without the full refactor. User will likely ask for: (1) OAuthService class with find-or-create-user logic (upsert on OAuth identity → user row, handling both new signups and linking to existing accounts), (2) Concrete OAuth provider implementations (GoogleOAuthProvider, GitHubOAuthProvider with exchange_code and token refresh), (3) Flask callback route (/auth/callback) that handles the OAuth code exchange and session creation, (4) Integration test showing the full flow from Google/GitHub OAuth initiation through user creation/linking.
